passages.explanation: The Pharisees and some teachers of the law who've come from Jerusalem gather around Jesus and notice that some of his disciples eat with hands that are defiled — unwashed. Mark explains for his Gentile readers: the Pharisees and all the Jews don't eat unless they give their hands a ceremonial washing, holding to the tradition of the elders. They observe many other traditions regarding the washing of cups, pitchers, and kettles. The Pharisees ask Jesus: why don't your disciples live according to the tradition of the elders? Why do they eat with defiled hands? Jesus replies with Isaiah 29:13: these people honour me with their lips, but their hearts are far from me. They worship me in vain — their teachings are merely human rules. You have let go of the commands of God and are holding on to human traditions. He gives an example: Moses said honour your father and mother. But you say that if someone declares that what might have been used to help their father or mother is Corban (a gift devoted to God), then you no longer let them do anything for their parents. You nullify the word of God by your tradition that you've handed down. And you do many things like that. The Pharisees have built a system of traditions that creates loopholes in God's actual commands. 'Corban' lets someone dedicate their money to the temple on paper while keeping it — and using religious devotion as an excuse not to care for elderly parents.

Jesus calls the crowd and says: listen to me, everyone, and understand this. Nothing outside a person can defile them by going into them. Rather, it's what comes out of a person that defiles them. When he leaves the crowd and enters the house, his disciples ask about the parable. He says: are you so dull? Don't you see that nothing that enters a person from the outside can defile them? It doesn't go into their heart but into their stomach, and then out of the body. Mark adds: in saying this, Jesus declared all foods clean. Then Jesus says: what comes out of a person is what defiles them. For it's from within, out of a person's heart, that evil thoughts come — sexual immorality, theft, murder, adultery, greed, malice, deceit, lewdness, envy, slander, arrogance, and folly. All these evils come from inside and defile a person. This is revolutionary — Jesus overturns the entire dietary purity system in a single statement. The problem with humanity isn't contamination from the outside; it's corruption from the inside. The heart, not the menu, is the issue.

Jesus leaves and goes to the vicinity of Tyre. He enters a house and doesn't want anyone to know, yet he can't keep his presence secret. A woman whose little daughter has an impure spirit hears about him and falls at his feet. She's a Greek, born in Syrian Phoenicia. She begs Jesus to drive the demon out of her daughter. He says: first let the children eat all they want, for it isn't right to take the children's bread and toss it to the dogs. She replies: Lord, even the dogs under the table eat the children's crumbs. He tells her: for such a reply, you may go — the demon has left your daughter. She goes home and finds her child lying on the bed, and the demon gone. Jesus' statement about children and dogs reflects the priority of his mission — Israel first. The woman doesn't argue with the priority; she argues with the limitation. Her faith isn't offended by the metaphor — it works within it and finds a way in. And Jesus honours her wit and persistence.

Jesus leaves Tyre, goes through Sidon, and down to the Sea of Galilee into the region of the Decapolis. There some people bring a man who is deaf and can hardly talk, and they beg Jesus to place his hand on him. Jesus takes him aside, away from the crowd. He puts his fingers into the man's ears. Then he spits and touches the man's tongue. He looks up to heaven and with a deep sigh says: Ephphatha! — which means 'be opened!' The man's ears are opened, his tongue is loosened, and he begins to speak plainly. Jesus commands them not to tell anyone. But the more he does so, the more they keep talking about it. People are overwhelmed with amazement: he has done everything well — he even makes the deaf hear and the mute speak. Jesus' method here is unusually physical — fingers in ears, spit, touch. For a man who can't hear, Jesus communicates through what he can feel. The sigh suggests the emotional weight of the healing. And the crowd's verdict echoes Genesis 1: God saw everything he had made, and it was very good. Jesus is making things right again.
